children.name: Locustella
children.description: Secretive streaked warblers of dense vegetation producing sustained mechanical insect-like songs. The Grasshopper Warbler can reel continuously for over two minutes. They are among the most difficult European birds to see, creeping through vegetation like small brownish mice.

children.name: Locustella naevia
children.description: A skulking, streaky warbler whose reeling, insect-like song can continue unbroken for minutes from deep within rank vegetation. It is far more often heard than seen.

children.name: Locustella fluviatilis
children.description: An eastern warbler with a rhythmic, mechanical, sewing-machine song, favouring damp scrub and waterside thickets. It reaches the western edge of its range in Sweden, where it is a scarce but regular breeder.

children.name: Locustella luscinioides
children.description: A reed-bed warbler whose buzzing, insect-like reel is lower and faster than the Grasshopper Warbler's, delivered from dense reed and sedge. A scarce breeder in southern Swedish wetlands.

children.name: Locustella lanceolata
children.description: A highly skulking warbler of dense grass and sedge, heavily streaked above and below. It breeds across the taiga zone from Scandinavia to the Russian Far East and winters in South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Locustella certhiola
children.description: A large, well-marked Locustella of marshy grasslands and reedy floodplains across Siberia, wintering in the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. It has a distinctive rufous rump and well-streaked breast.

children.name: Locustella ochotensis
children.description: A large, bulky Locustella breeding in tall grasses and scrub on the coasts and islands of the Russian Far East, wintering in Southeast Asia. Named for the Sea of Okhotsk region where it is common.

children.name: Locustella pleskei
children.description: A Vulnerable species that breeds on coastal islands off Japan, Korea, and northeastern China, wintering in the Philippines and Vietnam. Population is declining due to habitat loss at breeding and wintering sites.

children.name: Locustella fasciolata
children.description: One of the largest Locustella, breeding in dense forest undergrowth and tall grass in Siberia and the Russian Far East. It winters in the Malay Archipelago and is notably secretive for its size.

children.name: Locustella davidi
children.description: A small, compact bush warbler of dense undergrowth along forest margins in central and southern Siberia, wintering in southern China and Indochina. It was named in honour of French missionary-naturalist Pere Armand David.

children.name: Locustella thoracica
children.description: A montane bush warbler of the Himalayas and southwestern China, inhabiting rhododendron scrub and subalpine meadows at high elevations. Its spotted breast distinguishes it from allied species.

children.name: Locustella mandelli
children.description: A warm russet-brown bush warbler of forest undergrowth and bamboo from the Himalayas to southern China and Taiwan. It was named after Louis Mandelli, Italian tea planter and ornithologist in northeast India.

children.name: Locustella luteoventris
children.description: A plain, buff-bellied bush warbler inhabiting dense undergrowth and bracken on hillsides from the Himalayas to northern Indochina. It is resident throughout its range and rarely moves far from thick cover.

children.name: Locustella tacsanowskia
children.description: A secretive, unstreaked bush warbler of dense scrub and tall grass in central and eastern China. Named after Polish zoologist Wladyslaw Taczanowski, it winters in Southeast Asia.

children.name: Locustella major
children.description: A large, long-billed bush warbler restricted to dense scrub along streams and in subalpine zones of the western Himalayas. It is rarely seen but detected by its loud, repeated song from deep cover.

children.name: Locustella montis
children.description: An endemic bush warbler of montane forest edges and grasslands on Java and Bali, Indonesia. It occupies dense herbage at elevations above 1,000 m and has a thin, high-pitched reeling song.

children.name: Locustella alfredi
children.description: A secretive warbler of dense bamboo thickets and montane undergrowth in the highlands of eastern Africa, from Ethiopia to Tanzania. Its song is a high-pitched insect-like trill, and it is far more often heard than glimpsed among the bamboo stems.

children.name: Locustella accentor
children.description: A montane bush warbler endemic to the highlands of northern Borneo, where it inhabits mossy forest undergrowth and dense herbage on Mount Kinabalu and neighbouring peaks. Its song is a distinctive series of clear descending whistles.

children.name: Locustella castanea
children.description: A chestnut-brown bush warbler endemic to the montane forests of Sulawesi, Indonesia. It skulks in dense understorey vegetation and is detected mainly by its thin, high-pitched insect-like song.

children.name: Locustella musculus
children.description: A recently described bush warbler endemic to Seram Island in the Moluccas, Indonesia. It inhabits montane forest undergrowth and is known only from a few locations on the island's higher slopes.

children.name: Locustella disturbans
children.description: A little-known bush warbler endemic to Buru Island in the Moluccas, Indonesia, where it frequents dense undergrowth in montane forest. Its vocalisations and ecology remain poorly documented due to the remote nature of its habitat.

children.name: Locustella caudata
children.description: A large, strikingly long-tailed bush warbler endemic to montane forests of Sulawesi, Indonesia. It creeps through tangled undergrowth and mossy branches, its graduated tail feathers giving it a distinctive silhouette.

children.name: Locustella kashmirensis
children.description: A recently split bush warbler of the western Himalayas, breeding in dense rhododendron and birch scrub at high elevations in Kashmir and Himachal Pradesh. It winters at lower altitudes in the Himalayan foothills.

children.name: Locustella alishanensis
children.description: An endemic bush warbler restricted to montane forests of Taiwan, particularly in the Alishan range. It inhabits dense understorey thickets and bamboo stands, where its simple insect-like song betrays its presence.

children.name: Locustella idonea
children.description: A bush warbler found in the Langbian Plateau of southern Vietnam, inhabiting dense scrub and grass tussocks in montane areas around Da Lat. It was split from the Russet Bush Warbler complex based on vocal and genetic differences.

children.name: Locustella seebohmi
children.description: A bush warbler endemic to the montane forests of Luzon in the Philippines, occurring primarily in the Benguet province. Skulking and elusive, it inhabits dense undergrowth and breeds at elevations above 1,500 m.

children.name: Locustella chengi
children.description: A recently described bush warbler from the mountains of central China, named after Chinese ornithologist Cheng Tso-hsin. It inhabits dense scrub and bamboo thickets in Sichuan and neighbouring provinces, with a song distinct from the similar Spotted Bush Warbler.

children.name: Locustella portenta
children.description: A Vulnerable bush warbler discovered relatively recently on Taliabu Island in the Sula Archipelago, Indonesia. It inhabits montane forest undergrowth and is threatened by ongoing habitat loss on this single small island.

children.name: Megalurus
children.description: Large grassbirds of grasslands and reedbeds in Australasia and South and Southeast Asia. They are long-tailed, streaky brown birds that skulk through dense vegetation and are more often heard than seen.

children.name: Megalurus palustris
children.description: A large, boldly streaked grassbird of tall grasses, reedbeds, and scrub from India to the Philippines and Sulawesi. Its loud, far-carrying song is one of the most distinctive sounds of Asian wetland margins.

children.name: Megalurus punctatus
children.description: A nationally vulnerable New Zealand endemic inhabiting dense swamp vegetation and scrubland. Its distinctive filamentous tail feathers and reluctance to fly make it one of New Zealand's most secretive birds.

children.name: Megalurus gramineus
children.description: A small, streaked grassbird of freshwater and brackish swamps and reedy margins throughout mainland Australia. Its mournful, descending whistle is a characteristic sound of Australian wetlands.

children.name: Cincloramphus
children.description: Songlarks and tawny grassbirds of open grasslands and grassy woodlands in Australia and Southeast Asia. Males of the Australian songlarks perform distinctive aerial song-flights over their territories.

children.name: Cincloramphus cruralis
children.description: A large, nomadic Australian grassland bird with pronounced sexual dimorphism - the male is nearly twice the size of the female. Males perform conspicuous aerial song-flights above open grassy plains and paddocks.

children.name: Cincloramphus mathewsi
children.description: A rufous-rumped songlark of open woodlands, scrublands, and grasslands across most of mainland Australia. Males deliver a loud, rich song from exposed perches or in undulating song-flights.

children.name: Cincloramphus timoriensis
children.description: A large, tawny-streaked grassbird of tall grasslands, reedbeds, and rank herbage from the Lesser Sundas to northern and eastern Australia. It sings persistently from exposed grass stems and low shrubs.

children.name: Cincloramphus bivittatus
children.description: A thicketbird endemic to the Lesser Sunda Islands of Indonesia, where it inhabits dense undergrowth in forest and scrub on Timor and neighbouring islands. It has prominent buff wing-bars and a richly patterned underside.

children.name: Cincloramphus grosvenori
children.description: A Vulnerable thicketbird endemic to the island of New Britain in Papua New Guinea. It inhabits montane forest undergrowth above 1,000 m and is threatened by deforestation across its restricted range.

children.name: Cincloramphus llaneae
children.description: A Near Threatened thicketbird found only on Bougainville Island in Papua New Guinea. It skulks in the dense understorey of montane forest and is known from only a few locations on the island.

children.name: Cincloramphus macrurus
children.description: A large, long-tailed grassbird of open grasslands and savannas across New Guinea and the Aru Islands. It skulks in tall grass but occasionally perches up to sing its distinctive bubbling song.

children.name: Cincloramphus mariae
children.description: A thicketbird endemic to New Caledonia, where it inhabits dense undergrowth in humid montane forests. Its secretive habits and remote habitat make it one of the least-known members of the genus.

children.name: Cincloramphus rubiginosus
children.description: A thicketbird found in the highlands of New Guinea, from the Snow Mountains to the central cordillera. Its rich rusty-brown plumage and preference for dense mossy forest undergrowth make it a challenge to observe.

children.name: Cincloramphus rufus
children.description: An Endangered thicketbird endemic to the island of Vanua Levu in Fiji, where it inhabits dense forest undergrowth. It has notably long legs and is threatened by habitat degradation and invasive predators.

children.name: Cincloramphus turipavae
children.description: A Near Threatened thicketbird restricted to the island of Guadalcanal in the Solomon Islands. It inhabits montane forest undergrowth on the slopes of Mount Popomanaseu and is threatened by habitat loss.

children.name: Cincloramphus whitneyi
children.description: A thicketbird endemic to the island of Espiritu Santo in Vanuatu, where it frequents dense undergrowth in montane forests. Named in honour of the Whitney South Sea Expedition, it was long known only from historical specimens.

children.name: Schoenicola
children.description: Broadtail warblers of African and Asian grasslands and marshes, characterised by their distinctively broad, graduated tails used in display. They inhabit tall grass and reed habitats.

children.name: Schoenicola brevirostris
children.description: A secretive warbler of moist grasslands and marshy areas in sub-Saharan Africa, named for its distinctively broad, fan-shaped tail spread in display. It creeps through dense grass stems and is rarely seen in the open.

children.name: Schoenicola platyurus
children.description: A Near Threatened Indian endemic restricted to tall grasslands along the Western Ghats escarpment and a few sites in the northeastern hills. Its broad tail and preference for wet grasslands make it unmistakable.

children.name: Schoenicola striatus
children.description: A Vulnerable grassbird of tall grasslands in the Indian subcontinent, from Nepal and northern India to Bangladesh. It has distinctive bristly feathers around the bill and is threatened by loss of its grassland habitat.

children.name: Bradypterus
children.description: Bush warblers and scrub warblers of Africa and Asia, inhabiting dense vegetation near water and in montane forests. They are difficult to observe but produce loud, distinctive songs.

children.name: Bradypterus baboecala
children.description: A common and widespread African reed warbler inhabiting dense reedbeds, bulrushes, and rank sedge along rivers, lakes, and swamps from sub-Saharan Africa to South Africa. Its loud, accelerating rattle is distinctive.

children.name: Bradypterus lopezi
children.description: A montane bush warbler of dense undergrowth and bracken-fern in the highlands of central and eastern Africa, from Cameroon to Tanzania. It is resident on isolated mountain massifs and rarely descends to lower altitudes.

children.name: Bradypterus sylvaticus
children.description: A Near Threatened South African endemic restricted to dense tangles along streams in evergreen coastal and montane forests of the southern Cape, KwaZulu-Natal, and Swaziland. It is heard more readily than seen.

children.name: Bradypterus bangwaensis
children.description: A forest warbler restricted to the highlands of western Cameroon and adjacent Nigeria, where it inhabits montane forest undergrowth and bamboo thickets. It is named after the Bangwa region of Cameroon.

children.name: Bradypterus barratti
children.description: A warbler of montane forest edges and dense bracken in southeastern Africa, from Zimbabwe and Mozambique to eastern South Africa. It is named after the South African farmer and ornithologist Charles Barratt.

children.name: Bradypterus brunneus
children.description: A dark brown emutail endemic to the montane forests of eastern Madagascar, where it inhabits dense undergrowth and forest floor vegetation. It has a short tail and a furtive, mouse-like behaviour on the ground.

children.name: Bradypterus carpalis
children.description: A striking swamp warbler with prominent white patches in the wing, found in papyrus swamps and dense wetlands of the Great Lakes region of central Africa. It ranges through the eastern DRC, Uganda, Rwanda, and Burundi.

children.name: Bradypterus centralis
children.description: A recently split species from the Little Rush Warbler complex, found in montane wetlands of central Africa. It inhabits dense rushes and sedges in highland marshes from Ethiopia to Tanzania.

children.name: Bradypterus cinnamomeus
children.description: A warm cinnamon-coloured warbler of montane bracken and undergrowth in eastern and central Africa, from Ethiopia to Zimbabwe. Its loud, sputtering song is one of the characteristic sounds of African mountain forests.

children.name: Bradypterus grandis
children.description: A Near Threatened warbler of dense undergrowth in swampy forest clearings of the Congo Basin, known mainly from the Dja River region of Cameroon. It is among the largest and least-known Bradypterus species.

children.name: Bradypterus graueri
children.description: A Vulnerable swamp warbler restricted to papyrus and reed swamps in the highlands of the Albertine Rift in central Africa. Named after Rudolf Grauer, it is threatened by wetland drainage and conversion for agriculture.

children.name: Bradypterus seebohmi
children.description: A greyish emutail endemic to the highland forests of Madagascar, where it is found in dense undergrowth along forest edges and clearings. Named after Henry Seebohm, it has a loud, ringing song that carries through the misty forests.

children.name: Robsonius
children.description: Ground warblers endemic to the Philippines, inhabiting dense undergrowth and forest floor vegetation in montane forests of Luzon and Mindanao. They walk and hop on the ground like thrushes.

children.name: Robsonius rabori
children.description: A Vulnerable Philippine endemic restricted to montane forest floor and dense undergrowth in the Cordillera Central of Luzon. Named after Filipino ornithologist Dioscoro Rabor, it is threatened by deforestation.

children.name: Robsonius sorsogonensis
children.description: A Near Threatened ground warbler endemic to the Bicol Peninsula of southeastern Luzon, Philippines. It inhabits the forest floor of lowland and montane forests, foraging among leaf litter for invertebrates.

children.name: Robsonius thompsoni
children.description: A ground warbler endemic to the Sierra Madre range of eastern Luzon, Philippines. It differs from its congeners in vocalisations and plumage, and is named after ornithologist Max Thompson.

children.name: Helopsaltes
children.description: Grass warblers formerly placed in Locustella, separated by genetic evidence and markedly different song structure. They produce varied, flowing warbling songs rather than the mechanical insect-like reeling of true Locustella.

children.name: Helopsaltes amnicola
children.description: A large grasshopper warbler breeding on Sakhalin Island, the Kuril Islands, and coastal Hokkaido, wintering in Southeast Asia. Formerly treated as conspecific with Gray's Grasshopper Warbler, it differs in song and morphology.

children.name: Helopsaltes pryeri
children.description: A Near Threatened grassbird of reedbeds and marshy grasslands in Japan, China, and the Russian Far East. Named after Henry Pryer, a British entomologist who collected in Japan, it has a distinctive short tail and bubbling song.

children.name: Poodytes
children.description: Small to medium grassbirds of Oceania, inhabiting wetlands, spinifex grasslands, and forest edges. The genus was resurrected following molecular studies that showed Megalurus was polyphyletic.

children.name: Poodytes albolimbatus
children.description: A Vulnerable grassbird restricted to reedbeds and swamp margins along the Fly River and other lowland rivers of southern New Guinea. It has a distinctive white belly and is threatened by habitat degradation.

children.name: Poodytes carteri
children.description: A small grassbird endemic to the spinifex grasslands of arid inland Australia, ranging through central and western Queensland to Western Australia. It is well-camouflaged among the harsh spinifex hummocks it inhabits.

children.name: Poodytes rufescens
children.description: An extinct fernbird endemic to the Chatham Islands of New Zealand, last recorded in the late 19th century. It was larger than its New Zealand relative and fell victim to introduced rats, cats, and habitat destruction.

children.name: Elaphrornis
children.description: A monotypic genus endemic to Sri Lanka, containing the Sri Lanka Bush Warbler. It is sister to the Bradypterus-Megalurus clade but differs in its relatively shorter tail, longer bill, and unmarked plumage.

children.name: Elaphrornis palliseri
children.description: A Near Threatened bush warbler endemic to the highlands of central Sri Lanka, found in dense forest undergrowth above 1,200 m. Its loud explosive song is often heard at dawn in Horton Plains, but the plain brown bird is rarely glimpsed.

children.name: Malia
children.description: A monotypic genus endemic to Sulawesi, Indonesia. The Malia is a babbler-like bird with olive-green plumage that was long misclassified among the babblers before molecular studies placed it in Locustellidae.

children.name: Malia grata
children.description: A medium-sized babbler-like bird endemic to the montane forests of Sulawesi, Indonesia, with olive-green plumage, a yellowish head and chest, and a pinkish-brown bill. It travels in small noisy groups through the forest canopy, foraging for insects.
